So in theory, it could be used with OpenAI models as well. We're going to need to do some scaffolding. We're going to need to set up PNPM. We're going to want to set up Electron. We're going to want to get the core application structure in place so that we can bring up the UI. We're going to want some plans for sub-agents to work on to build out the threading system, the harness that talks to the agent, the LLM services that we're using. We're going to have another plan that builds skills and plugins. We're going to want to reimagine the way that threads are handled. I don like that threads are attached to a directory at the start I think that you should be able to create a thread without a directory and you should be able to bring the thread into that directory So I think that we should have a couple of different tabs or ways to view threads. So you can view them by directory if you want to see the ones that have an association with a set of project directories that you have. But you also should be able to just sort by recents and have it show you enough information, enough context in that list to say, you know, thread XYZ is using directories OpenClaw and OpenClaw Codex App Server, for example. And you need to be able to see that at a glance. And when you click into it, you need to see if it's making PRs against one directory or the other or both, and maybe see if it has stacked PRs or anything like that. So I think we need to reimagine the way that threads are allocated or navigated, and we need to allow cross-project threads more easily. I don't think we're terribly worried about security because these models or these agents are really only usable if you operating in sort of full access mode But I guess we probably should have an ability to prompt anytime a command is run and or check it with a cop agent to see if we think it safe or not and only prompt when we think it's unsafe. So, you know, it'll catch things like rmrf slash, but it won't catch things like run npm view dive to get package information. Let's see, I think we want to have sort of one of our enemies here would be copy paste pre work work where the user has to go and download and clone a bunch of different repos. So I think we want to have a knowledge wiki, we don't want the user to be encouraged to set up sort of their own system prompts, I guess it would be where, you They have to go into settings and say, if you use Git, always use SSH and verify your signatures. We don't want them to be typing that. We want to be adding to our own memory system. And I think that we should expose it as a wiki that they can navigate and view and edit a little bit if they want to. But for the most part it a system that we maintain and we let them explore and then we can run searches on it So I think we want to have a way to search that both lexically and semantically There are some projects on the web that are doing this right now, so we may want to borrow some ideas from that. I'll give you some links for that when we get to those processes. Let's see. I might want to explore the idea of having the agent run with essentially no keys. So it has no API keys on disk other than for accessing the LLM model that it's using. But even if the agent wants to check in some files and sign them with SSH, it would need to ask to unlock the SSH key. So essentially you can have an SSH agent using one password and one password would prompt the user. The problem with that is because it's an external program like 1Password, an SSH agent, we can't really control where that prompting is happening. So it would really only pop up on the machine and it wouldn't really be associated with a thread. So we may want to consider maybe making an SSH agent or capturing an SSH agent that we could expose the prompt for the password

 within our agent application. I'm not guaranteeing that's something we want to do, but that is something that would wow people. So that's something we might look at. I'd like to potentially explore the idea of distributed model use. So that cop agent I talked about earlier could potentially be a very small local model that's checking for command safety. And then it's choosing to route most everything else to the data center agent, the actual work. But that might not be particularly safe because smaller local models can be dangerous when it comes to generating commands. So we might not want to do that. The streaming-sitemaps project took probably 2-3 months of dedicated work, with probably the majority spent on shutterstock-specifics such as validating the canonical URLs, fixing canconical URL generation bugs in the website, revalidating after fixes, etc.  In addition there was some time lost due to file descriptor leaks on web page validation and p-map and p-queue not doing what people think they do (resulting in unconstrained parallelism, causing long GC pauses and unpredictable performance).

The project was immediately re-used in a project to build a digital-asset-manager.

Here is some info I sent in an earlier questionarrie about the projects:

```
2. High-Performance Data Pipelines at Shutterstock - Designed and built @shutterstock/streaming-sitemaps to enable new content to appear in sitemaps within minutes of publication for improved SEO. The system processed a Kafka topic with 4 billion+ metadata change events into 10,000+ packed XML sitemaps per language - representing 450 million items × 30 languages (13.5 billion total items written). Introduced AWS Kinesis for fine-grained concurrency control without modifying the upstream Kafka topic, ran 160 concurrent Lambda functions at sustained 100% CPU utilization with linear scaling through precise batching and outstanding request management, and created the open-source @shutterstock/p-map-iterable concurrency library to maximize throughput while applying back-pressure to prevent unbounded memory growth. This cleared the massive backlog in just a few hours and has remained current with incoming updates for years with almost no maintenance. The resulting Kafka → Kinesis → controlled-concurrency pattern was then applied to a behind-schedule Digital Asset Manager project, accelerating the processing of 10 billion Kafka messages from an estimated 2 years to under 2 months.
```

I think we want to cover this with CARL:

Context: Shutterstock wanted to recover lost SEO market share where Google clicks were increasingly going to competing websites instead of to Shutterstock.  A long-held belief of the Product team was that we needed to have all of our single-asset details pages in XML sitemaps so Google could crawl and index all 450 million of them multiplied by 30 languages and that this would assist Google in surfacing our content and bringing users to our site.  The belief was based on historical correlation between when clicks were higher and the fact that we had such a streaming sitemap system at the time, plus was backed up by advice from several SEO consultents that we should have every page in an XML sitemap.  I volunteered for this project.

Action: Lot of notes above, but the gist is that I built this entire system myself.  It read from an existing Kafka stream with 128 shards which was not correctly sized for ongoing operations (too many shards).  While a current-state DB may have been nice for creating the backfill, it turned out that the system of record for asset state was the Kafka Topic and the way to get the current state, as advised by the owning team, was to read the compacted stream in order.  An advantage of using hte Kafka Topic as the source of truth is that wiritng the code to handle the backfill would also achieve the streaming updates that were desired, while using two data sources would require some significantly different code to handle just hte backfill.  Kinesis was used as an intermediary because there was a need to control the number of shards in the topic - kinesis gave us a way to increase fan out during backfill and to them bring it down to 2-4 streams that kept up with incoming changes.  This limited down the number of files that were being read/appended to significant since each stream owns it's own file.  Kafka Topics cannot have their topic shard count changed after creation and the initial 128 shards would be adequate or too few in the beginning and 30x too many in the steady state.

Result: The system worked.  It backfilled the 3-4 billion messages for 450 million assets into ~10k XML sitemap files in a few hours.  We posted the sitemaps and then quickly follwed up with 30 more that had 450 million items for each language variant of the pages.  We monitored what happened to clicks.  The result was not what Product had intended and SEO consultants had suggested would happen.  For a period of time, nothing happened, then Google did start crawling pages rapidly for a few weeks or months, then Google slowed down on crawling again, our indexed page count went up then back down, and it appeared to not have the desired effect.

Learnings: Google does not appear to want sitemaps with 450 million items in them, particularly when those pages are not very distinquishable (such as stock photos).  Perhaps for discussion forums the results are quite different as there is a significant variation in the text on each page.  We re-examined the initial premise and found that there was strong evidence that we should reduce our indexed page count dramatically and that for e-commerce sites Google prefers to bring users to a search results page for "best cat photo" instead of to a specific asset page for "best cat photo" (as they cannot really tell what the best photo is and trying to chose from 10 million options is not something Google is good at).

The requirements and proposed architecture for handling control/experiment groups in `api-search` and `search-grpc` are as follows:

**Core Goal**
The primary objective is to avoid triggering a call to Open Search when possible.

**Interaction and Allocation**

  * **Allocation Location:** `search-grpc` (referred to as search tier PC) is currently responsible for allocating the user into an experiment or control group, though moving this boundary up is a possibility.
  * **Experiment Check:** When `API search` receives a query and identifies an active experiment for that integration, it does not know if the experiment applies to the specific query. Therefore, it must call `search-grpc` to ask if the `ping back ID` should receive the experiment or the control. The initial call to `search-grpc` for this metadata check might be unavoidable.

**Caching Strategy (DynamoDB)**

  * **Excluded Queries:** For queries that are determined to be excluded (meaning they do not exercise the experiment path), `API search` should write the *same* result into both the experiment and the control records in DynamoDB. A metadata flag should be added to indicate that the query was not part of the experiment.
  * **Experiment/Control Records:** Based on the allocation status received from `search-grpc`, `API search` should load the corresponding control record or experiment record from DynamoDB.

**Final Query Execution**

  * If the query needs to be run (it's not cached), `API search` calls `search-grpc` a second time, explicitly stating whether it wants to run the experiment or the control version.

My advice:

Use ECR pull-through cache for Docker Hub now, and pair it with a centralized Testcontainers image-prefix override. That is the fastest path with the least code churn.

What AWS supports today:

Yes, ECR now has pull-through cache rules for Docker Hub.
You create a pull-through cache rule in your private ECR registry.
You then pull via your ECR private URI, not the original Docker Hub URI.
On first pull, ECR creates the repository and caches the image.
Later pulls check upstream and refresh at least once every 24 hours.
If refresh fails, ECR can still serve the last cached image.
Sources:

Creating a pull through cache rule in Amazon ECR
Pulling an image with a pull through cache rule in Amazon ECR
Sync an upstream registry with an Amazon ECR private registry
Important caveat:

You cannot just keep saying docker pull mysql:8.0.32 and expect ECR to intercept it.
You must pull the ECR path, for example:
134757864544.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/docker-hub/library/mysql:8.0.32
For Docker Hub official images, /library/ is required.
For non-official Docker Hub repos, you omit library.
That means the answer to “can we always use ECR and have it fetch/cache automatically?” is:

yes, operationally
but only if you rewrite the image reference to the ECR pull-through namespace
For Testcontainers, that rewrite can be centralized:

Testcontainers supports TESTCONTAINERS_HUB_IMAGE_NAME_PREFIX
or hub.image.name.prefix in testcontainers.properties
Official docs:

Image name substitution
Image registry rate limiting
That is the key here. It means for Docker Hub images you likely do not need to edit every Scala test.

Recommended setup:

Create an ECR pull-through cache rule for Docker Hub with a prefix like docker-hub.
Store Docker Hub credentials in Secrets Manager for the rule, so ECR itself is authenticated upstream.
Ensure ARC runners can pull from private ECR.
Set TESTCONTAINERS_HUB_IMAGE_NAME_PREFIX=134757864544.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/docker-hub/ in the Scala test jobs.
Add Docker login to private ECR in CI if needed for the runner daemon.
That covers these common images:

mysql:8.0.32
localstack/localstack:4.5.0
redis:7.0.11-alpine
testcontainers/ryuk
opensearchproject/opensearch:*
It does not cover:

docker.elastic.co/elasticsearch/elasticsearch-oss:6.8.0
docker.elastic.co is not one of the ECR pull-through upstream registries AWS documents. For that one you would need to mirror it intentionally into ECR and then either:

update code to use the ECR image directly, or
add a custom Testcontainers image substitutor for that registry too

do Docker Hub pull-through first
wire Testcontainers to use the ECR prefix centrally
keep a short explicit allowlist of mirrored non-Hub images like docker.elastic.co/...
do not build a bespoke “check ECR, replicate if missing, rewrite dynamically” system unless you need it later

lowest code churn

AWS’s built-in option is Amazon ECR pull-through cache for Docker Hub:

you create a pull-through cache rule in ECR
ECR fetches and caches Docker Hub images on first pull
later pulls use your private ECR URI and refresh from upstream at least every 24 hours
for Docker Hub, AWS supports authenticated upstream access via Secrets Manager

134757864544.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/docker-hub/library/mysql:8.0.32
So the answer is:

yes to AWS-managed caching
no to a transparent Docker Hub mirror without changing the image reference

where would the mysql library get stroed in our ECR registry if we set this up?  Do they stay out of the way or do they get spinkled everywhere?
3:55 PM


They stay contained under the pull-through prefix you choose. They do not get sprinkled everywhere unless you choose a messy prefix.

If you create a Docker Hub pull-through rule with prefix docker-hub, then MySQL would land at:

134757864544.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/docker-hub/library/mysql:8.0.32
and the ECR repository name would effectively be:

docker-hub/library/mysql
For a non-official Docker Hub image like localstack/localstack:4.5.0, it would be:

134757864544.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/docker-hub/localstack/localstack:4.5.0
So the structure is predictable:

official Docker Hub images: <prefix>/library/<image>
other Docker Hub repos: <prefix>/<org>/<image>
That means if you choose a clean prefix like:

docker-hub
dockerhub-cache
third-party/docker-hub
then all of those cached repos stay grouped together.

I’d recommend a prefix like:

docker-hub
because it’s short and obvious.

So no, they won’t pollute the top level if you set the prefix sensibly. They’ll all live under one subtree in ECR.


3:55 PM
How do I know if something is official/unofficial when just looking at an image name/ That seems like a crazy arbitrary distinction that is not knowable without a lookup?
3:56 PM



You can usually tell from the image name syntax.

Rule of thumb:

mysql:8.0.32 is an official Docker Hub image because it has no slash
localstack/localstack:4.5.0 is non-official because it has an owner/org prefix
redis:7.0.11-alpine is official
testcontainers/ryuk:0.12.0 is non-official
So for Docker Hub:

no slash before the image name => official => ECR path needs /library/
has a slash like org/image => non-official => no /library/

mysql:8.0.32 -> .../docker-hub/library/mysql:8.0.32

if the image already has an explicit registry host like docker.elastic.co/..., don’t treat it as Docker Hub
